questions.question: Describe the four necessary conditions that must hold for a deadlock to occur.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode.
Hold and Wait: A process must be holding at least one resource and waiting to acquire additional resources held by other processes.
No Preemption: Resources cannot be preempted; they can only be released voluntarily by the process holding them.

Circular Wait: There must exist a set of waiting processes {P1, P2, ..., Pn} such that P1 is waiting for a resource held by P2, P2 is waiting for a resource held by P3, and so on, until Pn is waiting for a resource held by P1.

questions.question: Explain the primary difference between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance.
keyPoints: Deadlock Prevention: A set of methods that ensure at least one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock cannot hold, thus making the system deadlock-proof.

Deadlock Avoidance: Requires that the operating system have additional a priori information about the maximum resource needs of each process. The system then dynamically checks the resource allocation state to ensure a circular wait condition can never exist.
Key Difference: Prevention constrains resource requests to break a necessary condition, while avoidance uses knowledge of future requests to dynamically decide if a request can be safely granted.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Banker's Algorithm for deadlock avoidance with the Wait-Die and Wound-Wait schemes for deadlock prevention.
keyPoints: Banker's Algorithm (Avoidance): Requires knowledge of the maximum resource needs of all processes in advance. It uses a safety algorithm to check if granting a request will leave the system in a safe state (a state where deadlock cannot occur). It is a proactive, granting-time check.
Wait-Die (Prevention - Non-preemptive): If a process requests a resource held by a younger process, it will wait. If it requests a resource held by an older process, it will be aborted (die).

Wound-Wait (Prevention - Preemptive): If a process requests a resource held by a younger process, it preempts the resource (wounds the younger process). If it requests a resource held by an older process, it will wait.

Contrast: The Banker's Algorithm is a granting-time check for avoidance, while Wait-Die/Wound-Wait are timestamp-based ordering protocols for prevention, each with different rules for waiting and preemption/abortion.
